audit_status.review_note: Reviewed for test language, take-now beloved son formula, Moriah, burnt-offering terminology, fear-of-God, provided ram, YHWH-yireh, oath/blessing, and Nahor genealogy. Final source-critical review may refine Moriah/place-name and theological interpretation.

verses.restored: After these things, the Divine tested Abraham and said to him, Abraham. He said, Here I am.

familiar.text: And it came to pass after these things, that God did tempt Abraham...

notes.title: Tested, not tempted
notes.body: Nissah means tested or proved. The chapter is framed as a test, not enticement to evil.

notes.title: Here I am
notes.body: Hineni is availability and presence before a call.

verses.restored: He said, Take now your son, your only one, whom you love, Isaac, and go for yourself to the land of Moriah. Offer him there as a burnt offering on one of the mountains that I will tell you.

familiar.text: And he said, Take now thy son, thine only son Isaac, whom thou lovest...

notes.title: Your only one
notes.body: Yachid means only one, unique one, or beloved only. Ishmael still exists; the phrase centers Isaac as covenant son.

notes.title: Go for yourself
notes.body: Lekh lekha echoes Abram’s original call in Genesis 12.

verses.restored: Abraham rose early in the morning, saddled his donkey, took two of his young men with him and Isaac his son, split wood for the burnt offering, rose, and went to the place of which the Divine had told him.

familiar.text: And Abraham rose up early in the morning, and saddled his ass...

verses.restored: On the third day Abraham lifted his eyes and saw the place from afar.

familiar.text: Then on the third day Abraham lifted up his eyes, and saw the place afar off.

verses.restored: Abraham said to his young men, Stay here with the donkey. I and the boy will go over there; we will bow down, and we will return to you.

familiar.text: And Abraham said unto his young men, Abide ye here with the ass...

notes.title: We will return
notes.body: The plural return is important and theologically debated. The text leaves Abraham’s expectation unstated.

verses.restored: Abraham took the wood of the burnt offering and placed it on Isaac his son. In his hand he took the fire and the knife, and the two of them went together.

familiar.text: And Abraham took the wood of the burnt offering, and laid it upon Isaac his son...

notes.title: The two went together
notes.body: The repeated phrase emphasizes father and son walking in shared silence toward the test.

verses.restored: Isaac said to Abraham his father, My father. He said, Here I am, my son. He said, Look, the fire and the wood, but where is the lamb for the burnt offering?

familiar.text: And Isaac spake unto Abraham his father, and said, My father...

notes.title: Here I am, my son
notes.body: Hineni appears again, now in Abraham’s response to Isaac.

verses.restored: Abraham said, The Divine will see/provide for himself the lamb for the burnt offering, my son. And the two of them went together.

familiar.text: And Abraham said, My son, God will provide himself a lamb for a burnt offering...

notes.title: See/provide
notes.body: Raah can mean see, and in this context carries the sense provide. This wordplay becomes central in YHWH-yireh.

verses.restored: They came to the place of which the Divine had told him. Abraham built the altar there, arranged the wood, bound Isaac his son, and placed him on the altar upon the wood.

familiar.text: And they came to the place which God had told him of; and Abraham built an altar there...

notes.title: Bound Isaac
notes.body: Aqad means bind; this gives the traditional name Akedah, the binding.

verses.restored: Abraham stretched out his hand and took the knife to slaughter his son.

familiar.text: And Abraham stretched forth his hand, and took the knife to slay his son.

notes.title: Slaughter
notes.body: Shachat is the ritual slaughter verb. The text should remain stark here.

verses.restored: The messenger of the Eternal called to him from the heavens and said, Abraham, Abraham. He said, Here I am.

familiar.text: And the angel of the LORD called unto him out of heaven...

verses.restored: He said, Do not stretch out your hand against the boy, and do not do anything to him, for now I know that you fear the Divine, since you have not withheld your son, your only one, from me.

familiar.text: And he said, Lay not thine hand upon the lad, neither do thou any thing unto him...

notes.title: Do not stretch out your hand
notes.body: The command interrupts the act. The test ends with prohibition, not completion.

notes.title: Fear the Divine
notes.body: Fear here means reverent loyalty/obedience, not mere terror.

verses.restored: Abraham lifted his eyes and saw, and look, a ram behind him, caught in the thicket by its horns. Abraham went and took the ram and offered it as a burnt offering instead of his son.

familiar.text: And Abraham lifted up his eyes, and looked, and behold behind him a ram caught in a thicket...

notes.title: Instead of his son
notes.body: The ram replaces Isaac. The story turns from child-offering to provided substitute.

verses.restored: Abraham called the name of that place, The Eternal Will See/Provide. As it is said today, On the mountain of the Eternal, it shall be seen/provided.

familiar.text: And Abraham called the name of that place Jehovahjireh...

notes.title: YHWH-yireh
notes.body: The name plays on raah, see/provide. The familiar Jehovah-jireh is a later form of this name.

verses.restored: The messenger of the Eternal called to Abraham a second time from the heavens,

familiar.text: And the angel of the LORD called unto Abraham out of heaven the second time,

verses.restored: and said, By myself I have sworn, declares the Eternal, because you have done this thing and have not withheld your son, your only one,

familiar.text: And said, By myself have I sworn, saith the LORD...

notes.title: By myself I have sworn
notes.body: The oath is grounded in the Divine self, intensifying the promise.

verses.restored: I will surely bless you, and I will greatly multiply your seed like the stars of the heavens and like the sand that is on the seashore. Your seed shall possess the gate of its enemies.

familiar.text: That in blessing I will bless thee, and in multiplying I will multiply thy seed...

notes.title: Intensified blessing
notes.body: The Hebrew doubles the verbs: blessing I will bless, multiplying I will multiply.

verses.restored: In your seed all the nations of the earth shall be blessed, because you listened to my voice.

familiar.text: And in thy seed shall all the nations of the earth be blessed; because thou hast obeyed my voice.

notes.title: All nations blessed
notes.body: The universal blessing promise is reaffirmed through Abraham’s seed.

verses.restored: Abraham returned to his young men, and they rose and went together to Beersheba. Abraham dwelled in Beersheba.

familiar.text: So Abraham returned unto his young men, and they rose up and went together to Beersheba...

notes.title: Isaac not named in return verse
notes.body: The verse does not explicitly mention Isaac returning, though the broader narrative assumes his survival. The omission is literarily striking.

verses.restored: After these things, it was told to Abraham, Look, Milcah also has borne children to Nahor your brother:

familiar.text: And it came to pass after these things, that it was told Abraham...

notes.title: Rebekah setup
notes.body: The genealogy prepares for Rebekah’s appearance in Genesis 24.

verses.restored: Uz his firstborn, Buz his brother, Kemuel father of Aram,

familiar.text: Huz his firstborn, and Buz his brother, and Kemuel the father of Aram,

verses.restored: Chesed, Hazo, Pildash, Jidlaph, and Bethuel.

familiar.text: And Chesed, and Hazo, and Pildash, and Jidlaph, and Bethuel.

verses.restored: Bethuel fathered Rebekah. These eight Milcah bore to Nahor, Abraham’s brother.

familiar.text: And Bethuel begat Rebekah: these eight Milcah did bear to Nahor...

notes.title: Rebekah introduced
notes.body: Rebekah is named here before becoming central to Isaac’s story.

verses.restored: His concubine, whose name was Reumah, also bore Tebah, Gaham, Tahash, and Maacah.

familiar.text: And his concubine, whose name was Reumah, she bare also Tebah, and Gaham...

notes.title: Concubine
notes.body: Pilegesh means concubine, a secondary-status woman in the household structure.
